title: Spencer Cappelli -Hiring Manager Screen - Jason | SEO and GEO Lead
meeting_title: Spencer Cappelli -Hiring Manager Screen - Jason | SEO and GEO Lead

transcript.text: Hey, Spencer.
How's it going?
Doing pretty well.
How's it going?
Doing good.
Long week.
Actually, I thought it was a Friday for half a second, but I realize it's Thursday.
Oh, no.
That's like the worst feeling.
We have this webinar, which we always do very last minute.
We'll float tomorrow.
Once that's over, I'll be able to breathe a little bit.
about you?
Yeah, I hear you.
Yeah, just looking to enjoy the weekend.
I'm not sure where you're based, but we're here in upstate New York, and we're getting the nice foliage and wanting to be outside and do some hikes and stuff.
I'm in the Bay Area.
I'm in Oakland, and then I work in SF, but I'm from Toronto.
I don't know what it is with Asian parents and leaves turning yellow and red.
but they'll drive like two hours to go look at some trees.
I'm like, there's trees outside.
That's pretty funny.
Yeah, my wife is super into it too.
So we'll make some road trips and stuff for sure over the next couple of weeks.
Yeah, but it's great to meet you.
I got some context from the team on the conversation so far, but typically, I mean, we'd love to just share a little bit about what we're doing.
Not sure how much context you have.
And then also what we're trying to build, you know, with this role.
And then we'll have to hear more about you as well.
Does that sound good?
That sounds great.
Yeah, I've done some reading on the website, but it's always good to, you know, hear specifically about the systems and stuff and what you're trying to get angled towards.
So that'd be awesome.
Yeah, I think in general, you know, growthx, we're to our customers and agency, but what we're trying to build is essentially to productize content marketing, at least initially as a service.
So what we do for our customers generally is we'll own the chunk of their marketing function entirely that involves content marketing.
Everything from driving the strategy to creating content to measuring results and iterating.
And our whole angle is in 2025, if you're not using LLMs to at least create some leverage in that function of your company, you're leaving quite a lot on the table.
And I think that'll become increasingly more so.
So for our customers, we build machines that are kind of a mix of AI and people to create like certain types of content at an incredible scale, basically.
So like some examples, Webflow, you know, has an integrations part to their website that they just launched.
And essentially it's like, I mean, I think everyone knows that like Zapier play of like, you know, add a bunch of tools together.
But theirs is essentially almost like.
Developer docs for every kind of, like, everything basically you can even build with Webflow and another tool.
So, like, for example, the Stripe one, if you Google Webflow Stripe, we, like, give a bunch of options for, you know, how you can integrate them together.
And then what we're launching soon, because they liked it so much, is, you know, there's a section on that page where we have, like, some ideas for what you could do with it.
So, like, for example, hey, you can build the membership website with Webflow and Stripe, right, where you, like, paywall people and there's a subscription.
And now we're creating kind of the almost, like, documentation guides on, like, how you would build that.
And their goal with that is, like, you know, Webflow historically has been, like, pretty weak on the developer side.
Like, they're really strong designers and we're trying to help them essentially fix that.
So, in their case, we're, like, replacing, you know, honestly, like, a team of DevRel people.
Like, they're telling me, yeah, like, without you guys, we'd be writing, like, two or three of these.

And I think at the moment we're doing like 30 to 50 of these articles, you know, because essentially we've built a machine and then there's places in this machine where we have like actual developers to make sure it's right.
Yeah.
So, yeah.
So that's what, I mean, that's what growthx does.

I think we're pretty kind of bullish on marketing, just being completely changed in a variety of ways by AI, like whether that means, you know, what we're doing can last forever, which I don't think it can, like in terms of just dumping content, even if it is really high quality on the internet.
Like we're, our goal is to be at the forefront of that for our clients.
And then what I do here is I lead, I do growth and marketing.
So, you know, because of, I guess what we do as a company, that also means owning a bunch of clients, like Webflow and Surge and Augment Codes, the third one.
And then I also spend a bunch of time just like at the company level marketing what we do.
Um, which I don't know if you've seen, but like involves a lot of, you content podcasts, um, and things like that, but also involves kind of like figuring out how our service should evolve.
Um, so, you know, six months ago when I joined like GEO and AEO wasn't even really a thing that, um, we did.
I think some people maybe use the acronym a little bit and now it's like, you know, I think almost become the focus.
And I think it's just like pushing that further and further, um, is, is my goal.
And, and then this role, um, I, I think is, is meant to own a significant piece of that, like both the SEO and the GEO part.

So like on the SEO side, I mean, I think the playbook there though, it's changing is like, you know, relatively like set at least a little bit, you know, and it's just like, how do we kind of bring up the average quality of service across all of our customers?
Cause like not.
Everybody on the team is an SEO expert, right?
And we need somebody to kind of like centrally own that.
And then on the GEO side, it's the same thing, but maybe a little bit more experimental.
I think for the most part, nobody really knows how to even optimize for this stuff yet.
And it's like being essentially at the forefront of figuring that out, writing about it, and also getting our whole team kind of to adopt it together with me and some other people on the team.
Yeah, so that's kind of the role we're trying to hire for.
And then, I mean, I guess if you see my LinkedIn, I won't waste too much time, but like my background's basically had a growth for a couple startups.
I've been working on AI since 2018 for this company called Kite, which is almost identical to my current customer, Augment Code.
And it's interesting to see kind of the progression of AI.
And then I was also a founder.
And I met Marcel at a dinner like over a year ago with people that were like...
Kind of early on using AI for marketing.
Yeah, joined him a few months after, basically.
That's really cool.
Yeah.
I know he's from Brazil.
I was speaking to Marilia, who was the recruiter who basically did my first kind of phone screen.
And we had fun talking about that a little bit because before even my marketing career, I had a background in languages and studied Spanish, studied Portuguese.
And, you know, I was just talking to her about Brazilian music, which I thought she looked at me and she was like, did not think you were going to say those artists that you did.
So, yeah, no, that's fun.
It seems like a great culture, great people.
And it's really fun to kind of, you know, hear you unpack a little bit what you guys are building and what you're angling towards.
Yeah, for sure.
Great culture.
I would say, like, it's interesting.
Like, I don't think any agency, like regular growth agency could build.
Build a team like this, because for the most part, I would never join an SEO agency, because it's not interesting.
The outcomes aren't there, but what we're doing here is kind of dialing that up, and I think it brings together some really cool people.
Yeah.
Yeah, so it's a great team.
Yeah, I totally agree.

Yeah, read the VentureBeat article about Marcel kind of talking through the philosophy of the team, and it's one of the reasons why I was really bullish on starting this interview process and applying was because the idea that AI is here to basically augment expert judgment.

And I think that when you're in a world now where the barrier to produce content is so low, anyone can hop into Claude Sonnet or ChatGPT and they can produce a blog, having that editorial judgment combined with a systems thinking approach around AI is how brands are going to stand apart.
Um, and so felt very aligned on that and excited to, to learn more, but yeah.
And let me know if you have any questions.
I mean, we'd love to hear more about you for sure.
You know, I know a little bit about, about Tuff, uh, just from Googling it, but yeah.
I'd love to kind of hear your background as well.
Yeah, absolutely.
Um, so Spencer Cappelli, um, I've been doing SEO for about six, seven years.
I started doing marketing and in college, even for, uh, like the college of health sciences at the, at the university I went to in Ohio from Cleveland originally now in upstate New York.

Um, and yeah, I've done stints, uh, in-house, um, most recently was at, uh, AppQs where I was the content marketing manager, kind of in charge of SEO, mostly focusing on the blog and like distribution of, uh, learning content.
Uh, but I've also have significant agency experience four plus years, um, kind of how I got into the industry.
I had, uh, a journalism background and a writing background and a guy hired me because.
He was like, we can teach you SEO, you know, you know how to write and you know how to do editorial, and so we can teach you the rest.

And kind of since then, I've started from someone who was like an editorial first, kind of like content first person to someone who has helped manage content migrations, indexing issues for websites with a million plus pages, and kind of bringing that up to speed.

I, you know, most recently at Tough Growth, where I, you know, typically worked with a range of like five to seven clients, but often a lot more when we're talking about like new business opportunities, SEO audits, worked with brands like AARP, did audits for companies like Planned Parenthood, and then ran also SEO content strategies for companies like Multiverse in the UK.
And so I've, you know, I've been doing SEO enough to see, you know, when I started and how everyone was talking about like the arrival of semantic SEO, and where to where we are now, where.
And I have also gone kind of through like the phases of like existential dread where I was like, my God, like AI is going to replace all of us by like the end of 2025.
That's kind of how I felt like a year ago to where I am now, where I think that I do view the human input to being essential in making good content, especially when you can marry that to systems.

And, you know, playing around with tools, you know, understanding how to gauge and how clients value, you know, things like citations, you know, just their visibility in AI platforms, kind of translating that into a tangible business outcome.
So I would, I would probably agree with your assessment of agency life where a lot of it is kind of boring and rote.
And also sometimes you don't get to stick around long enough to prove those results out over a long period of time, which is frustrating.
It's probably the worst part about agency life, but I do think it was a valuable experience for me getting placed in front of so many different, like, you know, whether it's a V.
So I felt like I got a lot of great reps and experience doing that, especially over the last two years of my career.
I mean, I'll just say, I think agency is actually pretty interesting.
I think more of the point I was trying to make is, we have the head of design from Grammarly, we have early engineers from some of the unicorns here.
And I was saying more like, there's no way those people would ever join an SEO agency, but they would join us and build these incredible systems.
So I think categorically, there's maybe one or two agencies in the world that could try to do what we're trying to do, just by function of the team they can assemble.
And then, yeah, but I mean, for the most part, I think it's like super stressful, to be honest.
This is my first like kind of, you know, time.
second time.
Like add an agency essentially, but it's also, yeah, it's also fun.
Like you get to meet a lot of people, like you said.
Yeah, I do think that if like your previous experience is in-house, that transition can be very stressful.
We were kind of like in the beginning feeling like you're always on and kind of like, especially like the end of quarters.
I don't know if you experienced that too, but it's like, you know, everyone's trying to ship at the end of quarters.

Everyone's preparing like, you know, QPS docs and you've got like, you know, all these different clients that are trying to accomplish similar things, but with different goals and, you know, it's, it, that is an adjustment period.

So I think like having that agency experiences has been valuable in my career because of, you know, coming from in-house, there's a lot of like context change compared to when you're like, you're at a SaaS company and you're like, here's, you know, one product, three to four different target audiences, you know, your 10 battle cards that you need against competitors and like, go make content with that.
So definitely, definitely a change, but a good experience.
Is your client.
It's now mostly like consumer e-commerce, B2B, SaaS.
Do they follow like any sort of pattern?
Yeah, I'd say that the majority of the clients that I've worked with during my time at Tuff were B2B, SaaS, a healthy mix of e-commerce, more than a few ed tech companies as well.
You know, and we usually got like a, you know, because we were basically a division of a much larger kind of like prestige agency, I guess you could call it that.
They'd have been around a long time, Goodway Group, you know, and they were really focused on like programmatic media and activation for like big brands, like think Petco, General Motors.
And so occasionally, you know, I would get tapped to like come in and do like work on RFPs for organic for some of those bigger brands.
But I would say for the most part, most of the clients I've worked with were like in that B2B SaaS kind of like growth stage and more rarely like on the enterprise level.
But yeah, was kind of the sweet spot for me.
These giant agencies are super fascinating.
Do you have much interaction with the main company, like I guess the parent company?
have to be totally transparent.
I was actually impacted by essentially a restructuring at the company just a little over 10 days ago.
So I'm kind of looking for my next opportunity.
But that's kind of part of a function of like how messy all of this was.
Like I think they're essentially moving in a direction where they're not doing organic anymore.
And so, you know, that didn't augur well for what I did.
But yes, we would like get called in to, to interact with some of these companies and very like ad hoc ways, which was both like terrifying and exciting.
But yeah, talk to a bunch of different types of clients, which was good, I guess.
So like, I guess for the clients that you did have was...
Like optimizing for AI a big part of the engagements?
Or is that kind of still early?
It increasingly was.
One of the pushes that we had made in recent months was this idea of OneSearch is what like some of the Goodway leadership was branding it as.
Because most of their active accounts revolved around like paid and programmatic media and activation.

And so they were trying to sell them on, you know, kind of like the arrival of GEO as an inflection point in an opportunity to bring organic and LLM search into one umbrella, combining it with insights from like paid.
So, you know, taking a holistic approach to SERPs, you know, in general from paid results down to organic results, and then how that, you know, kind of leveled up to LLM visibility.
And it was really funny because they came up with kind of this proof of concepts and they were like, okay, you need to go in and like make it make sense.
And so I was just kind of thrown in front of these clients and was building doc.
I mean, this is probably.
Most of what I did for like the last month and it was starting to get some traction.
did not think it would, but then this restructuring happened.
So yeah, it was an interesting time.
Got it.
So you weren't actually like, were you guys like actively, you know, like monitoring prompts and like kind of trying to create content optimizing.
Did you guys do any of that?
For sure.
Yeah.

So like there were, I guess, two different types of clients, like the tough clients where I had actively like integrated GEO visibility into part of like our work and the other ones where it was more like audit and opportunity based.
But, you know, I guess for that first group, you know, our, our tool preference was profound.

And so, you know, kind of all the things you can get from that a typical workflow would be like, you know, here are some like branded non-branded keywords, you know, keywords that are like, you know, trying to find a good mix between like long tail that are maybe more like Bofu focused and keywords that are, you know, maybe mid funnel or above that higher search volume.
And then integrating those into the prompts that we were basically setting up for ProFound to execute across all the different LLM platforms to benchmark visibility and citations.
And so, yeah, I mean, it was a weird thing because I was reaching a point where I was starting to include this like in monthly reporting and deliverables, even when clients weren't asking for it.
Some of them were, but other ones I, you know, I just had a conversation and I was like, look, I know we're not even like charging for this yet, but we're building out this, you know, workflow.
And, you know, as an SEO professional, I honestly would feel remiss if I didn't mention that.
feel like everything about what we're doing right now is changing and you need to be aware of this.
I think like early days, we were also doing this and the tools early days were like incredibly expensive.
think ProFound was charging like three to five grand to track like a few hundred prompts.
We had a sweet, hard deal with them somehow.
Like we convinced them to, because I think they wanted like an agency to test them out.
So they allowed us for like $500 a month to put like 10 to 15 different websites into and like with different prompts.
But like it was highly problematic for what you said.
Like we didn't want to make a lot of noise in the account because we didn't want them to start charging us.
And so like we would go in and like remove ones that like didn't sign with us or like ones that didn't see.
Yeah, like we were also, yeah, man, I'm curious how you guys structured it.
But like we were also like, yeah, this is kind of expensive.
Like we should pass on the cost or should we just like wrap it and offer it?
And then like as we were thinking about that, the whole like bottom of that market just like fell through.
And now it's like whatever, 200 bucks, you know, for hundreds of prompts.
So now we don't really like we would just bundle it in.
Yeah.
But like what's your take on the whole just like LLM visibility, the kind of way it's done now?
Tracking prompts, you know, what you're doing to optimize.
Like do you have like just in general, I guess, what's your what's your thought on that whole space?
Yeah.

No, I think that probably my spiciest take, and this is not a dig at people who are building GEO, AEO strategies, I do think probably 80% of that work is what people who have been practicing good SEO for years would already have been baking into their workflows.

Like, I've seen too many LinkedIn posts from people who are like, you need to be using schema, and you need to be using, you should be like doing EAT, basically, should be doing EAT, like you need to be interviewing your experts.
I'm like, people have been doing this for years, like this is not, that's not GEO.
And so I think that's probably my biggest take, like there are a lot of people who are trying to pass this off as something that's entirely new, and it's not.
But that being said, do think that GEO, AEO, whatever you want to call it, is distinct enough insofar that there are different weights.
You know, there are different, like there are different indicators, like there's been, you know, Kevin Indig, who writes the growth memo, he did a webinar a couple months ago.
And he presented some pretty solid evidence that was like, you know, backlinks are not as important for LLM visibility.
And in fact, you know, now we're seeing that, you you can be cited in LLMs and, you know, you don't need to have a backlink or like it be actually linked.
The brand mentioned is what matters.

But he talks about things like word count and, you know, bolded paragraphs and essentially just like, you know, easy to understand structure so that LLMs can, you know, extract and synthesize from different, from different sources.
And so I think like, it's not entirely new, but there are different weights.

Whereas like, if you're looking at traditional SEO, you know, if you're doing SEO in 2020 and you're starting from like ground zero, like you're probably trying to like stand up a ton of like top of funnel informational articles.
And like, you're also maybe buying links from like link farms that like with websites that have like a 40 to 60 domain authority.
And you're like, this is doing SEO.
You know, I think some of that stuff is going away and it should.
So that's why I'm kind of bullish on all of this.
But I also think there's a lot of people who are trying to like pass off these like unique relevatory insights that are not necessarily unique or relevatory.
That's my hot take, but.
Yeah, I think I'm with you.

Like there's like tactics I'm sure will come and go and maybe at any point in time, like might give you some lift, but like the core of everything, at least to us, I think it's just like, I mean, like the purpose of all this content is to like provide value to somebody.
And like, that's, that's the benchmark, you know, and I think internally for us, like AI helps get there.

Cause like, I mean, again, like don't, don't blow your budget on writing words, you know, from somebody who's using chat GPT anyway, like spend your budget on interviewing people, doing surveys, like digging for like real information that's helpful, you know, testing products and doing research yourself.
Um, but I guess like, like, what about just like the monitoring itself?
Like, I think, um,
Like kind of pinging prompts and then seeing the response and then like tracking that.
Like, do you, do you have a take on that?
Like the efficacy of it essentially?
Yeah.

I mean, I guess the efficacy of like pinging these prompts, mean, you could using a tool like Profounder Evertune to do that is definitely going to be more efficient than trying to do like that sort of manual visibility benchmarking where you're like hopping into chat GPT and you're like, what are the test bend, what are the 10 best CRMs?
You know, I mean, I think that's one of the benefits of those tools is you, you do get the automation, the automation of like, you know, hundreds of those executions a day across different platforms.
I think it only works if you have a really good understanding of what prompts are most valuable to you.
And this is something that is still like, I'll be honest, like you mentioned like six to eight months ago, a lot of people were, were, were kind of working with geo for the first time.
Like this is one of the, maybe the dots I'm still trying to connect.
Like.
Like.
Like.
Like.
Because a lot of these tools, at least the last time I was in there, they don't offer an equivalent of a monthly search value for a prompt that you can find with a SEMrush keyword.
I think it makes sense.
All of these queries, they're not keywords.
Most of them are long-tail expressions, and they're very conversationally oriented on traditional keywords that people would type in Google.
And so I think that's one of the question marks for me is, how do you know which of the prompts is actually valuable?
You can use monthly search volume from an SEO tool as a proxy to understand if a prompt is valuable.
But outside of that, I think it's just having a really strong understanding of the ICP and the customer journey.
Because if you know how someone moves from lack of awareness down to conversion, then you can kind of model that out with the prompts that you select.
But, you know, like agencies, for example, might not always be the ones who are best positioned to do.
Do that, like, you know, or they have to really rely on someone who has a really strong understanding of the customer journey of the product.
So, yeah, all of this, I think it is valuable.
Like, you know, everyone is talking about it.

So it is, I think it's important to start benchmarking these things, but we can't also let it get into an area where, you know, like, I would never be on board with an SEO strategy, for example, that's entirely based on like, okay, how many non-branded organic keywords we have in the top 10 in SEMrush now?
Versus like, how are we actually driving business value and conversions and demos booked?
Like, there's a tendency to use these, like, proxy metrics as like the North Star when they probably shouldn't be.
So, long answer, but I hope that kind of touched on where you are.
No, I think that makes sense.
I think, I mean, that's been like one of our challenges of how to communicate this stuff.
Because depending on the client, some of them might almost obsess over like, hey, when I ask this in ChatGPT, like, I don't show up.
Like, how do we?
And fix that, right?
But then at the same time, it's like, you don't really know.
I mean, there's proxies for it, as you said.
Some of the prompts, I guess, are kind of close to like, you go to your favorite SEO tool and it's like, oh, there's questions in this kind of topic area.
And some of the prompts are kind of similar to that.
But for the most part, you know, like one, how to even guess at how the prompts are worded.
But, you know, and two, like most of them, like you wouldn't find with any search volume, like at least the SEO equivalent.
So we have a hard time kind of figuring out how to like, weigh the importance of it, you know, and also how to communicate it.
Did you have any kind of experience doing that?
sounded like you were being pushed to do it, you know?
I was, like I said, I kind of just like cobbled it together in the way that most made sense to me.
And I think it started, like, like, if we think about like, retrieval, you know, like LLMs, like.

They scan the open web, okay, so like we know that a lot of the same inputs for that, like what causes visibility in SEO to an extent when we're talking about like RAG in particular that can also drive visibility in LLMs.
And so my thought process was if I don't really have any other indicators as to what makes a prompt or the key or the queries within a prompt valuable, then I'm going to use other indicators.
And so essentially that would be like SEO data.
Like I would go into SEMrush and I would say, you know, are there long tail queries?
A lot of them were not, but like, are there like, what are, or I can even go to a website and say like, okay, what are their top 100 organic keywords?
Pull out a selection of them and see what kind of prompts are being reproduced on the other end.
Or like, you know, I would pull out keywords and then have the ChatPGPT format those into sentences or prompts that were, you know, conversational information intent driven for the most part.
And then run those through and see how the visibility was benchmarked on the other end.
And I think I recognize that's a very.
That was just kind of one of the ways that, that was one of the solutions I came up with when someone came to me and was like, hey, we need to do this.
I, you know, I think that, like, to your point earlier, like, we're all kind of learning how this works.

And, you know, I think the best thing that you can do as a practitioner of SEO and organic is be, you know, be voracious with, like, how you are reading what other people do with your own experimentation when possible.
Because, yeah, there's a lot of dots that still have be connected, in my opinion.
Is there, like, an experiment you would run to, like, try to figure more of this out?
Have you ever thought about, yeah, like, if you had, you know, resources to do that, like, what would be an interesting way to kind of unpack how all this works?
Yeah.
I mean, with resources, I mean, like, so if I think about probably Google Search Console, I think would play a big role.
Like, how are people actually landing on your website?
So you have, like, keywords.

And so I would think about some sort of system workflow that automates the communication between your SEMrushes, between your Profound, and between your Google Search Console instance, so you could pull in all of these different queries and try to map patterns, like, are there different ways, like, what are the queries that are actually generating click through?
So, like, queries with high CTR, you know, does that matter?
Does it not?
I don't know.
I feel like I would need to kind of experiment with it more, but those are the platforms that I'd be looking for, kind of that raw material.
And I think there was another thought, but I lost it.
Yeah, I that's probably where I'm at.
So I guess you're trying to kind of create a system to maybe help you almost, like, bring some, like, measurable quality to the prompts.
I guess it's, like, if you have a vote prompt, that's, like, I mean, for the most part,
Like what are the best CRMs I'm sure has the same as an SEO keyword, but if it was like, you know, what are the best ways to automate your outbound sales process?
Like that sentence probably does not exist in Sumrush, but like outbound sales automation and like there are like keywords that are kind of like related to that topic.
And maybe you can kind of map together some idea of like, hey, this prompt should have a little bit more weight than the other one.
That's like, you know, does your CRM integrate with Slack, which is like kind of important, but less so and probably converts less, right?
Like you're trying to kind of, yeah, like bring some volume and like conversion intent to like the prompts you're tracking.
Is that kind of what you're.
Yeah, I think this also, it kind of, it kind of resonates with like this off repeated claim that I've seen on LinkedIn.
That is like people who have set up their Google analytics so that they can track like LLM referrals.
I've seen a lot of people say that LLM traffic.
Is higher intent than organic.
And that's the kind of claim that myself as a skeptical person, I read that and I'm like, okay, but how and why?
How are you measuring that?

so maybe pulling in the actual click-through and combining it with SEMrush, Longtail, and then you have an agent that kind of interfaces between the two to kind of put stress tests on some of these claims.
I don't know.
I'm sure you've probably seen that claim as well.
Yeah, I mean, I've seen that one.
There's actually somebody on our team who's left now that was very adamant about that claim.
It's like, hey, if you look at the query lengths over a certain number, here are the kind of things people are typing into, like Gemini.
And I feel like that just kind of wasn't totally true.
And then the claim that triggers me the most is like when somebody pastes like a pie chart of like what the most cited sources are.
And...
And...
And they don't talk at all about like what the prompts that they're tracking are.
Because like for the most part, it's not like everyone has the same set of prompts, right?
So if you show me a pie chart, you're like, it's like, my God, it's like 70% Wikipedia and Reddit.
Like, I feel like I could make that any number depending on what prompts I chose, right?
